Astronomy Fact — A day on Venus is longer than its year: the planet rotates once every 243 Earth days but orbits the Sun every 225. Venus also spins backward, so the Sun rises in the west and sets in the east.

Biology Fact — The immortal jellyfish Turritopsis dohrnii can revert its adult cells back to a juvenile polyp stage through transdifferentiation, effectively resetting its life cycle. In principle it can repeat this indefinitely, escaping death by old age.

Physics Fact — A photon takes about 8 minutes to travel from the Sun to Earth, but a photon born in the Sun's core can take tens of thousands of years to reach the surface, bouncing in a random walk between dense plasma particles.

Chemistry Fact — Helium was discovered in the Sun before it was found on Earth. In 1868 astronomers spotted an unexplained yellow line in sunlight during an eclipse, naming the element after Helios, the Greek sun god, decades before it was isolated on Earth in 1895.

History Fact — The Library of Ashurbanipal at Nineveh, assembled in the 7th century BCE, held tens of thousands of clay tablets. Buried when the city fell in 612 BCE, the fire that destroyed Nineveh actually baked and preserved the tablets, including the Epic of Gilgamesh.

Math Fact — Zero is the only real number that is neither positive nor negative, and it took centuries to be accepted as a number. Indian mathematician Brahmagupta gave the first known rules for arithmetic with zero around 628 CE.

Art & Tech Fact — The oldest known figurative painting, a depiction of a wild pig found in the Leang Tedongnge cave in Indonesia, was dated in 2021 to at least 45,500 years old using uranium-series analysis of overlying mineral deposits.

Astronomy Fact — Neutron stars are so dense that a sugar-cube-sized piece of one would weigh about a billion tons on Earth. They pack more than the Sun's mass into a sphere roughly the size of a city, around 20 km across.

Biology Fact — Tardigrades survive being dried out by replacing lost water with a sugar-like molecule and special proteins that vitrify their cells into a glassy state. In this tun form they have endured the vacuum and radiation of open space.

Physics Fact — Helium is the only element that cannot be frozen solid at any temperature under normal atmospheric pressure. To solidify it you must apply about 25 atmospheres of pressure while cooling it near absolute zero.
Chemistry Fact — Hydrogen makes up about 90% of all atoms in the universe, yet it is so light that Earth's gravity can't permanently hold free hydrogen gas, which escapes to space over time.

History Fact — The Code of Hammurabi, carved around 1754 BCE in Babylon, contains 282 laws on a 2.25-meter basalt stele. The stele was later carried off as war booty to Susa in Iran, where French archaeologists unearthed it in 1901.
Math Fact — The number 1 is not considered prime, by convention. If it were, unique factorization would break, since 6 could be 2x3, or 1x2x3, or 1x1x2x3, and so on.

Art & Tech Fact — Johannes Vermeer's luminous interiors, painted in the 1660s, may have relied on a camera obscura; the technique's optical fidelity inspired modern study, and artist David Hockney advanced this 'optical' theory in his 2001 book Secret Knowledge.

Astronomy Fact — The Sun accounts for about 99.86% of the total mass of the entire Solar System. Everything else — all eight planets, moons, asteroids and comets — makes up barely a seventh of one percent.

Biology Fact — Your DNA, uncoiled and laid end to end from all your cells, would stretch roughly twice the diameter of the solar system. Inside each tiny nucleus, about two meters of it is packed into a space a few microns wide.

Physics Fact — When you stretch a rubber band quickly and press it to your lip, it feels warm; let it snap back and it feels cool. This is because rubber's entropy-driven elasticity makes it heat up when stretched and cool when relaxed.

Chemistry Fact — Gallium melts at about 30 degrees Celsius, so a solid chunk will turn to liquid in your warm hand. Despite this, it boils at over 2,400 degrees, one of the largest liquid ranges of any element.

History Fact — Egypt's Great Pyramid of Giza, completed around 2560 BCE, stood as the tallest human-made structure on Earth for nearly 3,800 years until Lincoln Cathedral's spire surpassed it around 1311 CE.
Math Fact — There are infinitely many primes, a fact Euclid proved around 300 BCE. His argument: multiply any finite list of primes and add 1; the result has a prime factor not on the list.

Art & Tech Fact — The first photograph from nature, 'View from the Window at Le Gras,' was made by Nicephore Niepce around 1826-1827 on a pewter plate coated with bitumen, requiring an exposure of several hours.
Astronomy Fact — Jupiter's Great Red Spot is a storm that has raged for at least 190 years, possibly much longer. It is shrinking, but is still wide enough to swallow the Earth whole.

Biology Fact — Octopuses have three hearts and blue, copper-based blood called hemocyanin. Two hearts pump blood through the gills while the third drives it around the body, and that main heart actually stops beating when the octopus swims.

Physics Fact — Sound travels roughly 4.3 times faster in water than in air, about 1,480 meters per second versus 343. In solid steel it moves even faster, near 5,960 meters per second, because tightly bound atoms transmit vibrations efficiently.

Chemistry Fact — Diamond and graphite are both pure carbon, yet one is the hardest natural material and the other is soft enough to write with. The difference is purely how the carbon atoms are bonded together.

History Fact — Cleopatra VII, who died in 30 BCE, lived closer in time to the first Moon landing in 1969 than to the construction of the Great Pyramid of Giza, which was already over 2,500 years old in her lifetime.

Math Fact — Twin primes are pairs differing by 2, like 11 and 13. Whether infinitely many exist is unproven, but in 2013 Yitang Zhang proved infinitely many prime pairs differ by at most 70 million, later reduced to 246.

Art & Tech Fact — The Antikythera mechanism, a Greek geared device for predicting astronomical positions, dates to roughly 100 BCE and is considered the oldest known analog computer; it was recovered from a shipwreck in 1901.
Astronomy Fact — Light from the Sun takes about 8 minutes and 20 seconds to reach Earth. So when you look at the Sun, you see it as it was over eight minutes in the past.

Biology Fact — The bacterium Deinococcus radiodurans can survive radiation doses thousands of times higher than would kill a human. It shatters and then meticulously reassembles its own genome, earning the nickname Conan the Bacterium.

Physics Fact — A spinning gyroscope resists changes to its orientation due to conservation of angular momentum. This is why a moving bicycle is far easier to balance than a stationary one and why spinning bullets fly straight.
Chemistry Fact — Water is one of the very few substances that expands when it freezes. Ice is about 9% less dense than liquid water, which is why ice floats and pipes burst in winter.

History Fact — The Rosetta Stone, discovered by French soldiers in 1799, repeats one decree in three scripts. It let Jean-François Champollion crack Egyptian hieroglyphs in 1822, ending roughly 1,400 years in which no one could read them.

Math Fact — The sum of the reciprocals of all primes diverges to infinity, even though primes thin out. Euler proved this, showing primes are in a sense denser than perfect squares, whose reciprocals sum to a finite value.
Art & Tech Fact — Leonardo da Vinci wrote most of his notebooks in mirror script, running right to left, so the text appears reversed and is most easily read by holding the pages up to a mirror.
Astronomy Fact — Saturn is the least dense planet in the Solar System, with an average density lower than water. If you could find a bathtub big enough, Saturn would float.

Biology Fact — A single teaspoon of healthy soil can contain more microorganisms than there are people on Earth. These bacteria, fungi, and protists drive the nutrient cycling that keeps land ecosystems alive.

Physics Fact — The color of gold comes from relativity. Its electrons move so fast that relativistic effects shift the metal's light absorption, giving gold its yellow hue instead of the silvery color of most metals.

Chemistry Fact — Mercury is the only metal that is liquid at room temperature, while bromine is the only nonmetal liquid. Both sit near each other in being the sole liquid elements under ordinary conditions.

History Fact — Hannibal led his Carthaginian army, including war elephants, across the Alps in 218 BCE to attack Rome from the north. Despite winning the Battle of Cannae in 216 BCE, where tens of thousands of Romans died, he never captured the city of Rome.
Math Fact — A Mersenne prime has the form 2^n minus 1. The largest known prime numbers are nearly all Mersenne primes because a fast test, the Lucas-Lehmer test, exists specifically for them.
Art & Tech Fact — The world's first programmer is widely considered Ada Lovelace, who in 1843 published an algorithm for Charles Babbage's Analytical Engine to compute Bernoulli numbers.
Astronomy Fact — There may be more stars in the observable universe than grains of sand on all of Earth's beaches. Estimates run to around 10^22 to 10^24 stars.

Biology Fact — Honeybees communicate the direction and distance of food through a waggle dance. The angle of the waggle relative to vertical encodes the flower's direction relative to the sun, and the dance duration encodes distance.

Physics Fact — Glass is not a slow-flowing liquid. It is an amorphous solid, and old cathedral windows are thicker at the bottom because of how medieval glassmakers cast and installed the panes, not because the glass sagged over centuries.

Chemistry Fact — The element francium is so radioactive and rare that at any given moment only a few grams exist naturally in the entire Earth's crust, and no one has ever seen a visible bulk sample of it.

History Fact — The Antikythera mechanism, a bronze geared device recovered from a Greek shipwreck around 1901, dates to roughly the 2nd century BCE. It modeled the positions of the Sun, Moon, and planets, and no comparable geared technology reappeared for over 1,000 years.
Math Fact — Goldbach's conjecture states every even number greater than 2 is the sum of two primes. It has been verified by computer past 4 quintillion but remains unproven after nearly 300 years.

Art & Tech Fact — Michelangelo painted the Sistine Chapel ceiling between 1508 and 1512, working on fresh plaster (buon fresco) standing on scaffolding, not lying on his back as the popular myth claims.
Astronomy Fact — A single teaspoon of the Sun's core is hot enough to kill a person from 150 km away. The core reaches about 15 million degrees Celsius.

Biology Fact — The axolotl can regrow entire limbs, parts of its heart, spinal cord, and even sections of its brain without scarring. Unlike most salamanders, it stays in its larval form for life, a condition called neoteny.

Physics Fact — Quantum tunneling lets particles pass through energy barriers they classically could not cross. This effect powers nuclear fusion in the Sun, since protons tunnel through their mutual electrical repulsion to fuse.

Chemistry Fact — A teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh billions of tons, but in ordinary chemistry, osmium is the densest natural element. A cube of it the size of a sugar cube weighs about 22 grams.

History Fact — Rome's Colosseum, inaugurated in 80 CE, could be drained and flooded for staged naval battles called naumachiae. It could seat an estimated 50,000 spectators and was funded partly with spoils from the Jewish-Roman War.
Math Fact — The prime number theorem says the number of primes below n is approximately n divided by the natural logarithm of n. It links the seemingly random primes to the smooth logarithm function.
Art & Tech Fact — The color 'mauve,' the first widely produced synthetic dye, was discovered by accident in 1856 by 18-year-old chemist William Perkin while trying to synthesize quinine.

Astronomy Fact — Olympus Mons on Mars is the tallest known volcano in the Solar System, rising about 22 km — roughly two and a half times the height of Mount Everest. Its base would nearly cover the state of Arizona.

Biology Fact — Mitochondria, the powerhouses of your cells, were once free-living bacteria engulfed by an ancestral cell roughly 1.5 billion years ago. They still carry their own circular DNA, inherited almost entirely from your mother.

Physics Fact — The Heisenberg uncertainty principle means empty space is never truly empty. Virtual particle pairs constantly flicker into and out of existence, producing measurable effects like the Casimir force between two close metal plates.

Chemistry Fact — Glass is not a slow-flowing liquid; it is an amorphous solid. Old cathedral windows are thicker at the bottom because of how medieval glass was made, not because the glass dripped over centuries.

History Fact — The eruption of Mount Vesuvius in 79 CE buried Pompeii and Herculaneum so completely that wooden objects, food, and even the shapes of victims were preserved. Plaster casts poured into voids in the ash reveal the exact poses of the dying.
Math Fact — 1729 is the Hardy-Ramanujan number, the smallest number expressible as a sum of two cubes in two different ways: 1 cubed plus 12 cubed, and 9 cubed plus 10 cubed.

Art & Tech Fact — The first message sent over ARPANET, the precursor to the internet, was attempted on October 29, 1969; the operators typed 'LOGIN' but the system crashed after just 'LO,' making 'lo' the first transmitted text.
Astronomy Fact — Voyager 1, launched in 1977, is the most distant human-made object, now over 24 billion km from Earth in interstellar space. Its radio signals take more than 22 hours to reach us.

Biology Fact — The bristlecone pine Methuselah in California is over 4,800 years old, making it one of the oldest known non-clonal living organisms. It was already ancient when the Great Pyramids were being built.

Physics Fact — Light slows down in glass and water, dropping to about two-thirds its vacuum speed in water. This change in speed bends the light's path, which is why a straw in a glass appears broken at the water's surface.
Chemistry Fact — Pure gold is so unreactive that it does not tarnish or rust, which is why ancient gold artifacts still gleam. It dissolves only in aqua regia, a mix of nitric and hydrochloric acids.

History Fact — The ancient city of Petra, capital of the Nabataeans, thrived as a trade hub by carving monumental facades into rose-colored cliffs. After Rome annexed it in 106 CE and trade routes shifted, the city slowly faded from Western knowledge until 1812.

Math Fact — Some infinities are bigger than others. Cantor proved the real numbers cannot be put in one-to-one correspondence with the counting numbers, so there are strictly more reals than integers.

Art & Tech Fact — Vincent van Gogh sold very few paintings during his lifetime, yet produced about 2,100 artworks, including roughly 860 oil paintings, most in the last two years before his death in 1890.

Astronomy Fact — The Moon is slowly drifting away from Earth at about 3.8 cm per year, roughly the rate your fingernails grow. Hundreds of millions of years ago, days on Earth were only about 18 hours long.

Biology Fact — Mantis shrimp punch with such speed that the water around their club briefly boils, creating cavitation bubbles whose collapse produces a second shock wave. The strike accelerates faster than a .22 caliber bullet.

Physics Fact — A teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh about a billion tons on Earth. Neutron stars are so dense that their atoms have collapsed, packing the mass of the Sun into a sphere only about 20 kilometers wide.

Chemistry Fact — The smell of rain has a name: petrichor. It comes partly from geosmin, a compound made by soil bacteria, which the human nose can detect at concentrations as low as a few parts per trillion.

History Fact — Emperor Ashoka of India, after the bloody Kalinga War around 261 BCE, renounced violence and embraced Buddhism. He had edicts promoting tolerance and welfare carved onto pillars and rocks across his empire, some still standing today.
Math Fact — The rational numbers, despite being dense, are countable: you can list every fraction in an infinite sequence. This means there are exactly as many fractions as whole numbers.

Art & Tech Fact — The QWERTY keyboard layout was designed by Christopher Latham Sholes in the early 1870s for the typewriter; one common explanation is that it spaced out frequently paired letters to reduce mechanical jamming.
Astronomy Fact — Black holes do not 'suck' things in; they have gravity like any mass. If the Sun were replaced by a black hole of equal mass, Earth's orbit would not change at all.

Biology Fact — Your body produces about 2.4 million new red blood cells every second to replace those that wear out. Each red blood cell circulates for roughly 120 days before being recycled by the spleen and liver.

Physics Fact — Absolute zero, minus 273.15 degrees Celsius, is the temperature at which atomic motion reaches its quantum minimum. It can never actually be reached, because removing the last bit of energy would require infinite work.
Chemistry Fact — Carbon dioxide has no liquid state at normal pressure. Solid dry ice sublimes straight to gas, which is why it produces fog without leaving a puddle behind.

History Fact — The Terracotta Army of China's first emperor, Qin Shi Huang, contains over 8,000 unique soldiers buried around 210 BCE. Farmers digging a well rediscovered the buried army by accident in 1974.

Math Fact — Cantor's diagonal argument constructs a real number missing from any proposed list of all reals, proving no such complete list can exist. It revolutionized our understanding of the infinite.

Art & Tech Fact — Pointillism, pioneered by Georges Seurat in works like 'A Sunday on La Grande Jatte' (1884-1886), relies on the eye optically blending small distinct dots of pure color rather than mixing pigment on the canvas.
Astronomy Fact — Mercury has ice at its poles despite being the closest planet to the Sun. Deep craters near its poles are permanently shadowed and never warm above freezing.

Biology Fact — The Pando aspen grove in Utah is a single male organism connected by one root system, with around 47,000 genetically identical trunks. Spanning over 100 acres, it is among the heaviest known living things on Earth.

Physics Fact — Lightning heats the surrounding air to roughly 30,000 Kelvin, about five times hotter than the Sun's surface. This sudden expansion of superheated air creates the shock wave we hear as thunder.

Chemistry Fact — Oxygen, the gas that keeps us alive, is also highly corrosive over time. The rusting of iron and the browning of a cut apple are both slow oxidation reactions driven by atmospheric oxygen.

History Fact — Chinese inventors had movable-type printing by around 1040 CE, when Bi Sheng made characters from baked clay. This predates Gutenberg's European printing press by roughly four centuries.

Math Fact — The continuum hypothesis asks whether any infinity sits strictly between the integers and the reals. Godel and Cohen showed it can neither be proved nor disproved from standard set theory axioms.
Art & Tech Fact — The first digital image was scanned by Russell Kirsch in 1957: a 176-by-176-pixel photo of his infant son, created on a computer at the US National Bureau of Standards.

Astronomy Fact — The largest known star, UY Scuti, is so vast that if placed where our Sun is, its surface would extend past the orbit of Jupiter. It would take light around 6 hours to travel across it.

Biology Fact — Some species of wood frog freeze almost solid each winter, with up to two-thirds of their body water turning to ice and their hearts stopping. They flood their cells with glucose as antifreeze and thaw back to life in spring.

Physics Fact — When you boil water on a mountain, it bubbles at a lower temperature because air pressure is reduced. At the summit of Everest water boils near 70 degrees Celsius, too cool to properly cook many foods.
Chemistry Fact — Table salt is made from two dangerous elements: sodium, a metal that explodes in water, and chlorine, a poisonous green gas. Bonded together, they form a substance essential to life.

History Fact — The Silk Road never carried a single caravan from China to Rome end to end; goods passed through many intermediary traders. The name 'Silk Road' itself was coined only in 1877 by German geographer Ferdinand von Richthofen.

Math Fact — Hilbert's Grand Hotel has infinitely many occupied rooms yet can still house a new guest: move everyone from room n to room n+1, freeing room 1. It illustrates the strangeness of infinite sets.

Art & Tech Fact — Katsushika Hokusai's 'The Great Wave off Kanagawa,' made around 1831, was a mass-produced woodblock print; thousands of impressions were pulled, so many original copies survive in museums worldwide.
Astronomy Fact — A year on Neptune lasts about 165 Earth years. Since its discovery in 1846, Neptune completed its first full orbit only in 2011.

Biology Fact — Sharks predate trees: the earliest sharks appeared over 400 million years ago, while the first true trees evolved around 350 million years ago. Sharks have survived all five mass extinctions since.

Physics Fact — Superconductors carry electric current with zero resistance below a critical temperature. A current started in a closed superconducting loop can persist for years without any measurable decay.
Chemistry Fact — The pH scale is logarithmic, so each step is a tenfold change. Stomach acid at pH 2 is about 100,000 times more acidic than pure water at pH 7.

History Fact — The Byzantine Empire deployed a mysterious incendiary weapon called Greek fire from the 7th century, which burned even on water. Its exact recipe was so secret that it has never been definitively reconstructed.

Math Fact — Pi is irrational, proven by Lambert in 1761, meaning its decimal expansion never ends or repeats. It is also transcendental, so it is not a root of any polynomial with integer coefficients.

Art & Tech Fact — Egyptian blue, used as early as around 3,250 BCE, is considered the first synthetic pigment; under near-infrared light it glows brightly, a property modern scientists now exploit to detect it in ancient artworks.
Astronomy Fact — The International Space Station orbits Earth roughly every 90 minutes, meaning its crew sees about 16 sunrises and 16 sunsets every single day.

Biology Fact — The human genome is about 8 percent ancient viral DNA, remnants of retroviruses that inserted themselves into our ancestors' cells. One such viral gene, syncytin, was repurposed to help build the placenta.
Physics Fact — Time runs slightly faster at your head than at your feet. Gravitational time dilation has been measured with atomic clocks separated by just 33 centimeters of height in a laboratory.

Chemistry Fact — Nitrogen makes up about 78% of the air you breathe, but plants and animals cannot use it directly. Only certain bacteria and industrial processes can break its triple bond to make it biologically available.

History Fact — Constantinople withstood numerous sieges behind its massive Theodosian Walls, built in the 5th century. The city finally fell in 1453 when Ottoman forces under Mehmed II used enormous cannons to breach the walls after over 1,000 years.
Math Fact — Euler's identity, e^(i*pi) + 1 = 0, links five fundamental constants: e, i, pi, 1, and 0. It is often cited as the most beautiful equation in mathematics.
Art & Tech Fact — The computer mouse was invented by Douglas Engelbart, who patented it in 1970; the first prototype was a carved wooden shell with two metal wheels, demonstrated publicly in 1968.
Astronomy Fact — Halley's Comet appears about every 76 years and last visited in 1986. It won't return to the inner Solar System until 2061.

Biology Fact — A lyrebird can imitate almost any sound it hears, including chainsaws, camera shutters, and other birds' songs. Males weave these mimicked sounds into elaborate displays to attract mates.

Physics Fact — The Doppler effect shifts the pitch of a passing siren and also the color of light from moving stars. Galaxies receding from us appear redshifted, which is how astronomers learned the universe is expanding.
Chemistry Fact — Tungsten has the highest melting point of any metal, around 3,422 degrees Celsius. This is why it was the classic material for the glowing filaments inside incandescent light bulbs.

History Fact — The University of al-Qarawiyyin in Fez, Morocco, founded in 859 CE by Fatima al-Fihri, is recognized by UNESCO and Guinness as the oldest existing degree-granting educational institution in the world.

Math Fact — The golden ratio, about 1.618, equals one plus its own reciprocal. It appears in pentagons, in the convergence of Fibonacci ratios, and is the irrational number hardest to approximate with fractions.
Art & Tech Fact — Andy Warhol created digital art on an Amiga computer in 1985 to promote the machine's launch; the files were lost for decades until recovered from old floppy disks in 2014.
Astronomy Fact — The footprints left by Apollo astronauts on the Moon will likely last millions of years. With no wind or liquid water, there is almost nothing to erode them.

Biology Fact — Cyanobacteria invented oxygen-producing photosynthesis and triggered the Great Oxidation Event around 2.4 billion years ago. They poisoned much of the existing anaerobic life but made complex life possible.

Physics Fact — A single bolt of lightning can carry a current of around 30,000 amperes and transfer enough energy to power a typical home for weeks, though most of it dissipates instantly as heat, light, and sound.
Chemistry Fact — Caffeine is a natural pesticide. Coffee and tea plants produce it to paralyze and kill insects that try to eat their leaves and seeds, and it happens to stimulate human brains too.

History Fact — Baghdad's House of Wisdom, flourishing from the 8th century, translated Greek, Persian, and Indian works into Arabic. Scholars there advanced algebra; the word itself comes from al-Khwarizmi's treatise 'al-jabr' written around 820 CE.
Math Fact — Euler's number e is the base rate of growth shared by all continually growing processes. It equals the limit of (1 + 1/n)^n as n grows without bound, roughly 2.71828.

Art & Tech Fact — The Lascaux cave paintings in France, created around 17,000 years ago, were discovered in 1940 by four teenagers and a dog; the cave was closed to the public in 1963 to protect the art from carbon dioxide and fungi.
Astronomy Fact — Pluto has not completed a single orbit since its discovery in 1930. One Plutonian year lasts about 248 Earth years.

Biology Fact — The blue whale's heart is about the size of a small car and can weigh around 180 kilograms. Its aorta is wide enough that a human could nearly crawl through it, and its heartbeat can be detected from miles away.

Physics Fact — Diamond is one of the best thermal conductors known, conducting heat about five times better than copper. This is why a real diamond feels cold to the touch as it rapidly draws heat from your skin.

Chemistry Fact — The fizz in soda is carbonic acid forming as carbon dioxide dissolves under pressure. When you open the can, the pressure drops and the gas escapes, which is why flat soda tastes slightly less sharp.

History Fact — Mansa Musa, ruler of the Mali Empire, made a pilgrimage to Mecca in 1324 carrying so much gold that his spending in Cairo reportedly depressed the region's gold value for years. He is often cited as one of the richest people in history.
Math Fact — The constant gamma, about 0.5772, measures the gap between the harmonic series and the natural logarithm. After centuries, no one knows whether it is even rational or irrational.
Art & Tech Fact — JPEG, the ubiquitous image compression standard, was issued in 1992 by the Joint Photographic Experts Group, whose name gives the format its acronym.
Astronomy Fact — The Milky Way and the Andromeda Galaxy are on a collision course, approaching at about 110 km per second. They are expected to merge in roughly 4.5 billion years.

Biology Fact — Plants can communicate distress through airborne chemicals and underground fungal networks. When attacked by insects, some emit volatile compounds that warn neighbors and even attract the predators of their attackers.

Physics Fact — Two beams of light can pass straight through each other without interacting, because photons do not carry electric charge. This is why crossed laser beams never deflect one another in ordinary conditions.
Chemistry Fact — Helium can leak through solid glass and many plastics because its atoms are so small. This is also why helium balloons deflate within a day or two even when perfectly sealed.

History Fact — The Great Zimbabwe stone city in southern Africa, built without mortar between roughly the 11th and 15th centuries, housed up to 18,000 people. Its massive curved walls gave the modern nation of Zimbabwe its name.

Math Fact — The square root of 2 is irrational, a discovery attributed to the Pythagoreans that allegedly so disturbed them that it became a closely guarded secret. It cannot be written as any ratio of integers.

Art & Tech Fact — The Mona Lisa was painted by Leonardo da Vinci beginning around 1503; its worldwide fame surged after it was stolen from the Louvre in 1911 by Vincenzo Peruggia, recovered two years later.
Astronomy Fact — Jupiter's moon Io is the most volcanically active body in the Solar System, with hundreds of active volcanoes. Some eruptions throw plumes hundreds of kilometers into space.

Biology Fact — Naked mole-rats almost never get cancer and can survive about 18 minutes without oxygen by switching to fructose-based metabolism like a plant. They also feel no pain from acid or chili-pepper capsaicin.

Physics Fact — The Pauli exclusion principle forbids two electrons from occupying the same quantum state. This single rule explains why matter takes up space and why atoms do not collapse into one another.

Chemistry Fact — DNA is held together by hydrogen bonds, the same weak attraction that gives water its surface tension. These bonds are weak enough to unzip for copying yet strong enough in number to keep the helix stable.

History Fact — The Kingdom of Aksum, in present-day Ethiopia, was among the first states to adopt Christianity, around the 4th century CE. It minted its own coins and erected towering carved granite obelisks, one of which stood over 24 meters tall.
Math Fact — A Mobius strip has only one side and one edge. If you cut it down the middle lengthwise, you do not get two strips; you get a single longer loop with two full twists.

Art & Tech Fact — The first webcam was created at the University of Cambridge in 1991 to monitor a coffee pot, so researchers could avoid walking to find it empty; it streamed online from 1993 until 2001.

Astronomy Fact — The temperature of the cosmic microwave background — the afterglow of the Big Bang — is about 2.7 degrees above absolute zero. It fills all of space and was emitted some 380,000 years after the universe began.

Biology Fact — The largest known organism by area is a honey fungus in Oregon's Blue Mountains, spreading across about 9 square kilometers underground. It is estimated to be between 2,400 and 8,650 years old.

Physics Fact — A gram of antimatter annihilating with a gram of matter would release energy comparable to a large nuclear bomb. Producing even a tiny amount of antimatter, however, currently costs astronomical sums of money.

Chemistry Fact — Stainless steel resists rust because of chromium, which forms an invisible, self-repairing oxide layer just a few atoms thick. Scratch the surface and the layer instantly reforms in air.

History Fact — The Bayeux Tapestry, an embroidered cloth nearly 70 meters long, depicts the Norman conquest of England in 1066. It also shows Halley's Comet, which appeared that year and was seen as an omen.

Math Fact — The Klein bottle is a surface with no inside or outside; it cannot exist without self-intersecting in our three-dimensional space. It is essentially two Mobius strips joined along their edges.

Art & Tech Fact — Yves Klein patented his own ultramarine shade, International Klein Blue, in 1960; he registered the formula to preserve the pure intensity of the pigment suspended in a special binder.
Astronomy Fact — Saturn's moon Titan is the only other body in the Solar System known to have stable liquid on its surface. But instead of water, it has lakes and rivers of liquid methane and ethane.

Biology Fact — Each of your cells contains telomeres, protective caps on chromosome ends that shorten with every division. The enzyme telomerase can rebuild them, and its activity is a key factor in both aging and cancer.

Physics Fact — Honey, water, and air all flow, but honey resists flow much more because of its high viscosity. Pitch, a tar-like substance, is so viscous it appears solid yet a famous experiment has watched it drip once a decade.
Chemistry Fact — Aerogel is up to 99.8% air, making it one of the lightest solids known. It is such a good insulator that a flower can sit on a thin slab while a blowtorch is held beneath it.

History Fact — The Domesday Book, completed in 1086 on William the Conqueror's orders, surveyed England's lands and holdings in such detail that it was used as a legal reference for centuries. Its name reflected that, like Judgment Day, its verdicts could not be appealed.
Math Fact — A sphere cannot be flattened onto a plane without distortion, which is why every world map distorts area, shape, or distance. This follows from Gauss's Theorema Egregium about curvature.
Art & Tech Fact — The Gutenberg Bible, printed around 1455, was the first major book produced in Europe with movable metal type; roughly 49 copies survive today, in whole or in part.

Astronomy Fact — The Sun converts about 4 million tons of mass into pure energy every second through nuclear fusion. Despite this, it has enough hydrogen fuel to keep shining for about 5 billion more years.

Biology Fact — The electric eel can generate discharges of up to 860 volts using stacked cells called electrocytes. It uses low-voltage pulses to navigate and locate prey, then high-voltage bursts to stun it.

Physics Fact — The greenhouse effect works because certain gases are transparent to incoming sunlight but absorb the infrared heat the Earth radiates back. Without any greenhouse gases, Earth's average surface would be far below freezing.
Chemistry Fact — The element bromine gets its name from the Greek word for stench. It is a deep red-brown liquid at room temperature that gives off choking, irritating vapors even when undisturbed.

History Fact — The Black Death reached Europe in 1347 aboard ships docking in Sicily and killed an estimated 25 to 50 million people within a few years. It may have wiped out a third to half of Europe's population.

Math Fact — The hairy ball theorem proves you cannot comb a hairy sphere flat without a cowlick. A practical consequence: there is always at least one point on Earth where the horizontal wind speed is zero.

Art & Tech Fact — The first photograph of a person was made by Louis Daguerre in 1838; a Paris boulevard scene that appears empty actually captured a man standing still to have his boots shined long enough to register.
Astronomy Fact — A spoonful of a white dwarf star would weigh several tons on Earth. These stellar remnants are Earth-sized but pack roughly the mass of the Sun.

Biology Fact — Cuttlefish can change the color and texture of their skin in milliseconds using pigment sacs called chromatophores, despite being colorblind. They may sense color through light receptors in their skin itself.

Physics Fact — When two pure tones with slightly different frequencies play together, you hear a throbbing called beats. The beat frequency equals the difference between the two tones, a trick musicians use to tune instruments by ear.

Chemistry Fact — Bananas are slightly radioactive because they are rich in potassium, and a small fraction of natural potassium is the radioactive isotope potassium-40. The dose is harmless and even used as an informal unit.

History Fact — Genghis Khan unified the Mongol tribes and by his death in 1227 had built the largest contiguous land empire ever assembled. The Mongols ran a relay courier system, the Yam, with way stations allowing messages to travel hundreds of kilometers per day.
Math Fact — Euler's formula for polyhedra states vertices minus edges plus faces equals 2 for any convex polyhedron. A cube gives 8 minus 12 plus 6, which equals 2.

Art & Tech Fact — Jackson Pollock's 'drip' paintings, made from 1947 onward, contain fractal patterns; physicist Richard Taylor showed in 1999 that their fractal dimension increased over the years of Pollock's career.
Astronomy Fact — Light from the nearest star beyond the Sun, Proxima Centauri, takes about 4.24 years to reach us. With current spacecraft, the journey would take tens of thousands of years.

Biology Fact — Bacteria in your gut outnumber your own cells and collectively weigh around 1 to 2 kilograms. They synthesize vitamins, train your immune system, and influence your mood through the gut-brain axis.

Physics Fact — The total angular momentum of a spinning skater is conserved, so pulling in the arms speeds up the spin dramatically. The same physics makes collapsing stars spin faster as they shrink into pulsars.

Chemistry Fact — Superglue bonds by reacting with trace moisture, including the humidity on your skin. The cyanoacrylate molecules rapidly link into long chains, which is why a tiny drop sets in seconds.

History Fact — Marco Polo's account of his travels to Asia, dictated around 1298 while he was imprisoned in Genoa, introduced many Europeans to the wealth of Kublai Khan's China. Some contemporaries doubted his tales, nicknaming him 'Il Milione.'
Math Fact — The four color theorem says any map can be colored with just four colors so no neighboring regions share a color. Proven in 1976, it was the first major theorem verified by computer.

Art & Tech Fact — The transistor, the building block of modern electronics, was invented at Bell Labs in December 1947 by John Bardeen, Walter Brattain, and William Shockley, who shared the 1956 Nobel Prize in Physics.

Astronomy Fact — Uranus is tipped on its side, rotating at an axial tilt of about 98 degrees. This means each of its poles gets around 42 years of continuous sunlight followed by 42 years of darkness.

Biology Fact — A hummingbird's heart can beat over 1,200 times per minute in flight, and it may visit a thousand or more flowers a day. At night some enter torpor, dropping their body temperature and heart rate to conserve energy.

Physics Fact — Radio waves, microwaves, visible light, X-rays, and gamma rays are all the same phenomenon: electromagnetic radiation differing only in wavelength. They all travel at exactly the same speed in a vacuum.

Chemistry Fact — Liquid nitrogen boils at minus 196 degrees Celsius, so it bubbles violently at room temperature. A drop poured on a warm floor skitters around on a cushion of its own vapor, the Leidenfrost effect.

History Fact — The Hundred Years' War between England and France actually lasted 116 years, from 1337 to 1453. Joan of Arc helped turn the tide for France before being captured and burned at the stake in 1431 at about age 19.

Math Fact — In hyperbolic geometry, the angles of a triangle sum to less than 180 degrees, and the deficit grows with the triangle's area. This consistent non-Euclidean geometry shattered the belief that Euclid's was the only one.

Art & Tech Fact — The Venus of Willendorf, a limestone figurine about 11 centimeters tall, was carved roughly 25,000-30,000 years ago and was found in Austria in 1908; traces of red ochre pigment remain on it.
Astronomy Fact — The supermassive black hole at the center of our galaxy, Sagittarius A*, has about 4 million times the mass of the Sun. The first direct image of it was released in 2022.

Biology Fact — The Venus flytrap counts to avoid wasting energy: it snaps shut only after two touches to its trigger hairs within about 20 seconds, and needs five total stimulations to begin secreting digestive enzymes.

Physics Fact — A mirror does not actually reverse left and right; it reverses front and back. The left-right flip you perceive is an illusion created by how you imagine turning to face your reflection.

Chemistry Fact — Ozone and ordinary oxygen are both made only of oxygen atoms, but ozone has three atoms per molecule instead of two. This small change makes ozone a pale blue gas with a sharp smell that shields Earth from UV.

History Fact — The Aztec capital Tenochtitlan, founded in 1325 on an island in Lake Texcoco, was one of the world's largest cities by 1500, with perhaps 200,000 residents. It was fed by chinampas, artificial floating garden plots in the lake.

Math Fact — The Banach-Tarski paradox shows a solid ball can be split into finitely many pieces and reassembled into two balls identical to the original. It relies on the axiom of choice and non-measurable sets.

Art & Tech Fact — The first message on the World Wide Web went live in 1991, but Tim Berners-Lee wrote the original proposal at CERN in March 1989; his boss famously noted it was 'vague but exciting.'
Astronomy Fact — Comet tails always point away from the Sun, no matter which way the comet is moving. The solar wind and radiation pressure push the tail outward.
Biology Fact — Sea otters keep kelp forests alive by eating sea urchins that would otherwise devour the kelp. As a keystone species, their presence or absence can flip an entire underwater ecosystem.

Physics Fact — The buoyant force on an object equals the weight of the fluid it displaces, a principle Archimedes discovered. A steel ship floats because its hull shape displaces a volume of water heavier than the ship itself.

Chemistry Fact — Iron is forged inside stars, but elements heavier than iron mostly require the violence of supernovae or merging neutron stars. The gold in your ring was likely made in a cosmic catastrophe.

History Fact — The Inca Empire built over 40,000 kilometers of roads through the Andes without the wheel or iron tools. Relay runners called chasquis carried messages and even fresh fish to the emperor across this vast network.
Math Fact — The birthday paradox: in a group of just 23 people, there is a better than even chance two share a birthday. With 70 people, the probability exceeds 99.9 percent.

Art & Tech Fact — Rembrandt's 'The Night Watch' (1642) was trimmed in 1715 to fit a wall in Amsterdam's town hall; in 2021 AI and a 17th-century copy were used to digitally reconstruct the cut-off portions.
Astronomy Fact — Mars appears red because its surface is covered in iron oxide — essentially rust. The fine dust gives the planet its distinctive reddish-orange color.

Biology Fact — Humans share about 60 percent of their genes with bananas, reflecting the deep common ancestry of all eukaryotic life. Core processes like DNA repair and energy metabolism are nearly universal.

Physics Fact — Sunlight reaching Earth's surface delivers about 1,000 watts per square meter at midday. The entire planet intercepts more solar energy in roughly one hour than human civilization uses in an entire year.

Chemistry Fact — Soap works because its molecules have a water-loving head and an oil-loving tail. They surround grease droplets with tails inward, forming tiny spheres called micelles that rinse away in water.

History Fact — Machu Picchu, built around 1450 high in the Peruvian Andes, was never found by Spanish conquistadors. It remained largely unknown to the outside world until American explorer Hiram Bingham publicized it in 1911.

Math Fact — The Monty Hall problem: after a host reveals a goat behind one of two unchosen doors, switching your pick doubles your odds of winning from one-third to two-thirds. It famously baffled professional mathematicians.
Art & Tech Fact — The smiley emoticon :-) was first proposed by computer scientist Scott Fahlman on a Carnegie Mellon message board on September 19, 1982, to mark jokes in plain-text posts.
Astronomy Fact — The fastest-spinning known pulsar rotates about 716 times per second, faster than a kitchen blender. Its equator moves at nearly a quarter of the speed of light.
Biology Fact — The Greenland shark can live over 400 years, making it the longest-lived vertebrate known. Individuals alive today may have been born before the founding of the United States.

Physics Fact — Electrons in a wire drift remarkably slowly, often less than a millimeter per second. The electrical signal itself, however, propagates near the speed of light because the electric field pushes all electrons nearly at once.

Chemistry Fact — Hydrofluoric acid is a weak acid by the textbook measure, yet it can dissolve glass and is dangerously toxic. It penetrates skin and disrupts the body's calcium, making it far more hazardous than its pH suggests.

History Fact — The Gutenberg Bible, printed around 1455 in Mainz, was the first major book produced with movable metal type in Europe. About 49 copies survive today, making them among the most valuable books in the world.
Math Fact — In any group of 6 people, either 3 mutually know each other or 3 are mutual strangers. This is the simplest case of Ramsey theory, which finds inevitable order in large enough structures.

Art & Tech Fact — Tutankhamun's burial mask, made around 1323 BCE, is crafted from about 10 kilograms of gold and inlaid with lapis lazuli, carnelian, and colored glass; it was discovered by Howard Carter in 1922.
Astronomy Fact — Earth is not a perfect sphere; it bulges at the equator due to its rotation. The diameter at the equator is about 43 km larger than from pole to pole.

Biology Fact — Slime molds have no brain or nervous system yet can solve mazes and recreate efficient transport networks. In one experiment, a slime mold reproduced the layout of Tokyo's rail system to connect food sources.

Physics Fact — Cold water can freeze faster than hot water under certain conditions, a counterintuitive phenomenon called the Mpemba effect. Its exact mechanisms remain debated, involving evaporation, convection, and dissolved gases.

Chemistry Fact — The noble gases were once called inert because they almost never react. Yet chemists have since forced xenon to bond with fluorine and oxygen, proving even the most aloof elements can be coaxed into compounds.

History Fact — When Christopher Columbus reached the Caribbean in 1492, he believed he had landed near Asia and called the inhabitants 'Indians.' He died in 1506 still convinced he had found a western route to the Indies.
Math Fact — Benford's law predicts that in many real datasets, the leading digit is 1 about 30 percent of the time, far more than the naive 11 percent. It is used to detect accounting fraud.

Art & Tech Fact — The first computer-generated film effects to win acclaim appeared in 1982's 'Tron,' but the movie was famously snubbed by the Academy for its visual effects because using computers was seen as cheating.
Astronomy Fact — The Hubble Space Telescope has traveled more than 7 billion km while orbiting Earth since 1990, all without ever leaving Earth orbit. It moves at about 27,000 km/h.

Biology Fact — The bombardier beetle defends itself by mixing chemicals in a reinforced chamber, producing a boiling, toxic spray ejected with audible pops. The reaction reaches around 100 degrees Celsius at the moment of firing.

Physics Fact — A rainbow is actually a full circle of light, but the ground usually cuts off the lower half. From an airplane or above a waterfall, you can sometimes see the complete circular rainbow.

Chemistry Fact — Chlorophyll and hemoglobin have nearly identical molecular structures, but chlorophyll holds a magnesium atom at its center while hemoglobin holds iron. That single difference makes one green and the other red.

History Fact — The Treaty of Tordesillas in 1494 split newly explored lands outside Europe between Spain and Portugal along a line in the Atlantic. This is why Brazil, falling on Portugal's side, speaks Portuguese while most of Latin America speaks Spanish.

Math Fact — The law of large numbers guarantees that averages converge to the true mean over many trials, but the gambler's fallacy wrongly assumes short runs must self-correct. A fair coin has no memory.

Art & Tech Fact — Frida Kahlo painted around 55 self-portraits out of her roughly 143 paintings, often explaining that she painted herself because she was so often alone and was the subject she knew best.
Astronomy Fact — Diamond rain may fall inside Neptune and Uranus. Extreme pressure can squeeze carbon from methane into solid diamonds that sink toward the cores.

Biology Fact — Roughly half the oxygen you breathe is produced by ocean phytoplankton, not land plants. Tiny photosynthetic organisms like Prochlorococcus may be the most abundant photosynthesizers on the planet.

Physics Fact — Entropy, the measure of disorder, almost always increases in an isolated system. This statistical tendency is why heat flows from hot to cold and gives time its apparent forward direction, the so-called arrow of time.

Chemistry Fact — Absolute zero, minus 273.15 degrees Celsius, is the temperature at which molecular motion reaches its minimum. It can be approached to within billionths of a degree but never actually reached.

History Fact — Ferdinand Magellan organized the first expedition to circumnavigate the globe in 1519, but he was killed in the Philippines in 1521. Only one of his five ships, the Victoria, completed the voyage home to Spain in 1522.
Math Fact — A normal distribution is fully described by just two numbers: its mean and its standard deviation. About 68 percent of values fall within one standard deviation of the mean.

Art & Tech Fact — The integrated circuit was independently invented around 1958-1959 by Jack Kilby at Texas Instruments and Robert Noyce at Fairchild Semiconductor; Kilby received the Nobel Prize in Physics in 2000.

Astronomy Fact — The largest known structure in the universe is the Hercules-Corona Borealis Great Wall, a vast filament of galaxies spanning billions of light-years. Its size challenges some assumptions about cosmic uniformity.

Biology Fact — Bats are the only mammals capable of true sustained flight, and many hunt using echolocation precise enough to detect a single hair-thin wire. Some produce calls louder than a rock concert, above human hearing.

Physics Fact — The strong nuclear force is about 100 times stronger than electromagnetism but acts only over distances smaller than an atomic nucleus. Beyond roughly a femtometer it effectively vanishes, which is why nuclei have size limits.

Chemistry Fact — The hottest chili heat is measured by capsaicin, a single molecule. Pure capsaicin is so potent that a few milligrams can cause intense pain, and it does not dissolve in water, which is why milk soothes the burn.
History Fact — Nicolaus Copernicus published his theory that the Earth orbits the Sun in 1543, the same year he died. Legend holds he received the first printed copy on his deathbed.
Math Fact — Buffon's needle: drop a needle on a floor of evenly spaced lines, and the probability it crosses a line involves pi. This lets you estimate pi by randomly tossing needles.

Art & Tech Fact — Damien Hirst's 'For the Love of God' (2007) is a platinum cast of a human skull set with 8,601 diamonds, including a large pink stone on the forehead, with an asking price reported around 50 million pounds.

Astronomy Fact — Saturn's rings are made mostly of water ice, ranging from dust-sized grains to chunks the size of houses. Despite spanning hundreds of thousands of km, they are often less than a kilometer thick.

Biology Fact — Your stomach lining replaces itself every few days because stomach acid is strong enough to dissolve metal. A protective mucus layer and constant cell turnover keep the organ from digesting itself.

Physics Fact — A black hole's event horizon is not a physical surface but a boundary of no return. For a black hole the mass of the Sun, this horizon would lie just about 3 kilometers from the center.
Chemistry Fact — Phosphorus was the first element discovered by a known person, in 1669, by boiling down large quantities of human urine. It glows faintly in air as it slowly reacts with oxygen.

History Fact — The Ottoman architect Mimar Sinan designed hundreds of structures over a career spanning the 16th century, including the Selimiye Mosque completed in 1575. He served three sultans and reportedly lived to about 99 years old.
Math Fact — Fermat's Last Theorem claims no whole numbers satisfy a^n + b^n = c^n for n greater than 2. Fermat scribbled it in a margin in 1637; Andrew Wiles finally proved it in 1994.
Art & Tech Fact — The first hard disk drive, IBM's RAMAC 305 of 1956, stored about 5 megabytes on fifty 24-inch spinning platters and was roughly the size of two refrigerators.
Astronomy Fact — Astronauts grow slightly taller in space, by up to about 5 cm. Without gravity compressing the spine, the discs between vertebrae expand; the effect reverses back on Earth.

Biology Fact — The arctic tern undertakes the longest known migration, flying from the Arctic to the Antarctic and back each year. Over a lifetime its travels can total over 2.4 million kilometers, several round trips to the moon.

Physics Fact — When light hits a soap bubble, waves reflecting off the front and back surfaces interfere, canceling some colors and reinforcing others. This thin-film interference creates the bubble's shifting iridescent swirls.

Chemistry Fact — Catalysts speed up reactions without being consumed. The catalytic converter in your car uses platinum, palladium, and rhodium to turn toxic exhaust gases into less harmful ones as they pass through.

History Fact — The Mughal emperor Shah Jahan built the Taj Mahal between 1632 and roughly 1653 as a mausoleum for his wife Mumtaz Mahal. He was later deposed by his son Aurangzeb and spent his final years imprisoned with a view of the monument.

Math Fact — The Riemann hypothesis, concerning the zeros of the zeta function, is the most famous unsolved problem in math. It carries a million dollar prize and would reveal deep structure in the distribution of primes.
Art & Tech Fact — The Terracotta Army of China's first emperor, buried around 210 BCE and rediscovered by farmers in 1974, includes over 8,000 soldiers, each with individualized facial features.

Astronomy Fact — Betelgeuse, the red supergiant in Orion's shoulder, is so large that if placed at the Sun's position it would engulf the orbit of Mars. It is expected to explode as a supernova within the next 100,000 years.

Biology Fact — DNA replication is astonishingly accurate, making only about one error per billion bases thanks to proofreading enzymes. This precision allows complex multicellular life to copy its blueprint reliably.

Physics Fact — The Earth's magnetic field is generated by the churning motion of molten iron in its outer core, acting like a giant dynamo. This field has reversed its north and south poles many times over geologic history.
Chemistry Fact — Titanium is as strong as many steels but far lighter, and it fuses well with bone. This biocompatibility is why it is used for hip replacements, dental implants, and surgical screws.

History Fact — The Tokugawa shogunate enforced a policy of national seclusion called sakoku from the 1630s, largely cutting Japan off from foreign contact. It lasted until US Commodore Matthew Perry's fleet forced trade open in 1853.
Math Fact — The Collatz conjecture: take any positive integer, halve it if even or triple it and add one if odd, and you always reach 1. It is verified to enormous numbers but remains unproven.
Art & Tech Fact — The GIF format was created by Steve Wilhite at CompuServe in 1987; he later insisted the word is pronounced with a soft 'g,' like the peanut butter brand, sparking lasting debate.
Astronomy Fact — The Andromeda Galaxy is the most distant object visible to the naked eye, about 2.5 million light-years away. The light you see from it left before modern humans existed.

Biology Fact — Some deep-sea anglerfish males permanently fuse to females, dissolving their own organs until they become little more than a sperm-producing appendage. The female may carry several attached males at once.

Physics Fact — A falling cat can right itself in midair without any external torque by twisting its front and back halves in opposite directions. It conserves zero net angular momentum while still flipping over to land on its feet.

Chemistry Fact — The Maillard reaction is why seared steak, toasted bread, and roasted coffee taste so rich. It is a complex web of reactions between amino acids and sugars triggered by heat, producing hundreds of flavor compounds.

History Fact — Galileo Galilei was tried by the Roman Inquisition in 1633 for supporting heliocentrism and spent his last years under house arrest. The Catholic Church formally acknowledged errors in his case only in 1992.
Math Fact — The Poincare conjecture, about characterizing the 3-sphere, was solved by Grigori Perelman in 2003. He declined both the Fields Medal and the million dollar Millennium Prize.
Art & Tech Fact — Salvador Dali collaborated with Walt Disney on a short film called 'Destino,' begun in 1945 but shelved; it was finally completed and released in 2003, decades after Dali's death.
Astronomy Fact — Jupiter has the shortest day of any planet, completing one rotation in under 10 hours despite being the largest. Its rapid spin flattens it noticeably at the poles.

Biology Fact — Trees in a forest share resources through underground fungal networks sometimes called the wood wide web. Older mother trees can funnel carbon and nutrients to struggling seedlings through these fungal connections.

Physics Fact — Microwave ovens heat food by making water molecules rotate rapidly as the alternating electric field flips them billions of times per second. The friction between jostling molecules is what produces the heat.

Chemistry Fact — A single gram of activated charcoal can have a surface area larger than a tennis court because it is riddled with microscopic pores. This vast area lets it trap toxins and odors by adsorption.

History Fact — The Great Fire of London in September 1666 destroyed much of the medieval city, including old St Paul's Cathedral, yet official records list only a handful of deaths. It also helped end a lingering outbreak of plague.

Math Fact — Godel's incompleteness theorems prove that any consistent mathematical system rich enough for arithmetic contains true statements it cannot prove. Mathematics can never be both complete and consistent.

Art & Tech Fact — The pixel-art video game character Mario was originally named 'Jumpman' and first appeared in the 1981 arcade game Donkey Kong; his cap and mustache were design solutions to the era's low resolution.

Astronomy Fact — The Voyager spacecraft each carry a Golden Record, a phonograph disc with sounds and images of Earth. It includes greetings in 55 languages and music ranging from Bach to Chuck Berry.

Biology Fact — The peregrine falcon is the fastest animal on Earth, reaching over 380 kilometers per hour in a hunting dive. Special baffles in its nostrils let it breathe against the immense air pressure of the stoop.

Physics Fact — Special relativity shows that mass and energy are interchangeable through E equals m c squared. The energy locked in a single gram of any matter equals roughly the explosive yield of 21,000 tons of TNT.
Chemistry Fact — Lithium is the lightest metal and floats on oil. Its eagerness to give up an electron makes it ideal for rechargeable batteries that power phones, laptops, and electric cars.
History Fact — Isaac Newton served as Warden, then Master, of the Royal Mint from 1696. He aggressively pursued counterfeiters, personally gathering evidence that sent several to the gallows.
Math Fact — The Fibonacci sequence, where each number is the sum of the two before, appears in sunflower seed spirals and pinecones. Consecutive Fibonacci numbers tend toward the golden ratio.
Art & Tech Fact — Color used in Renaissance paintings could signal wealth: ultramarine blue, ground from lapis lazuli imported from Afghanistan, was at times more expensive than gold by weight.

Astronomy Fact — Enceladus, a small moon of Saturn, shoots geysers of water vapor and ice from a global subsurface ocean through cracks at its south pole. This makes it a prime target in the search for life.

Biology Fact — Each human breath contains molecules once exhaled by people throughout history because air mixes so thoroughly over time. Statistically you inhale atoms that passed through countless other organisms before you.

Physics Fact — A perfectly black object would absorb all light and emit a characteristic glow based solely on its temperature. Stars approximate such blackbodies, which is why their color reveals how hot their surfaces are.
Chemistry Fact — Most of your body's mass is oxygen, mainly locked inside water molecules. By number of atoms, however, hydrogen wins, since water has two hydrogen atoms for every oxygen.

History Fact — Peter the Great of Russia traveled incognito through Western Europe in 1697-1698 to learn shipbuilding and crafts firsthand. He even worked in Dutch and English shipyards to bring those skills back home.

Math Fact — Pascal's triangle hides the Fibonacci numbers in its diagonals, the powers of 2 in its row sums, and the binomial coefficients in its rows. Coloring its odd entries reveals the Sierpinski triangle fractal.

Art & Tech Fact — The first email was sent by Ray Tomlinson around 1971; he chose the @ symbol to separate the user from the host machine because it was already on the keyboard and rarely used in names.
Astronomy Fact — A neutron star can spin up enormously when feeding off a companion. The matter falling onto it can heat to millions of degrees, blasting out X-rays detectable across the galaxy.

Biology Fact — The pistol shrimp snaps its claw so fast it creates a cavitation bubble that briefly reaches temperatures near that of the sun's surface. The collapse emits a flash of light and a sound loud enough to stun prey.

Physics Fact — Capillary action lifts water up a thin tube against gravity through surface tension and adhesion. This same effect draws water from a tree's roots toward its leaves, contributing to how tall trees stay hydrated.

Chemistry Fact — The element cesium reacts so violently with water that even a small piece can shatter its container with an explosion. It is also used in the atomic clocks that define the modern second.

History Fact — The smallpox vaccine was pioneered by Edward Jenner in 1796 when he inoculated a boy with cowpox to confer protection. Smallpox was declared eradicated by the World Health Organization in 1980, the only human disease so far eliminated.
Math Fact — A fractal has detail at every scale and often a non-integer dimension. The coastline of Britain has no fixed length: the finer your ruler, the longer it measures.

Art & Tech Fact — Banksy's 'Girl with Balloon' partially shredded itself moments after selling for about 1.04 million pounds at Sotheby's in 2018; the half-shredded work, renamed 'Love is in the Bin,' later sold for far more in 2021.
Astronomy Fact — There is a giant cloud of alcohol in space near the constellation Aquila. Sagittarius B2 contains vast amounts of ethyl alcohol and other complex organic molecules.

Biology Fact — Certain bacteria thrive in deep-sea hydrothermal vents at temperatures above 100 degrees Celsius, using chemosynthesis instead of sunlight. They form the base of entire ecosystems independent of the sun.

Physics Fact — Quantum entanglement links two particles so that measuring one instantly determines the state of the other, no matter the distance. This correlation cannot transmit information faster than light, so causality is preserved.

Chemistry Fact — Spider silk is a protein that, weight for weight, can be tougher than steel and stretchier than nylon. Its strength comes from crystalline regions linked by flexible, tangled protein chains.

History Fact — The metric system was born of the French Revolution, with France adopting the meter and kilogram in the 1790s. The meter was originally defined as one ten-millionth of the distance from the equator to the North Pole along the meridian through Paris.
Math Fact — The Mandelbrot set is generated by one simple rule, z becomes z squared plus c, yet its boundary is infinitely complex. Its boundary has a fractal dimension of exactly 2.

Art & Tech Fact — The Apollo Guidance Computer that helped land humans on the Moon in 1969 had about 4 kilobytes of RAM and ran at roughly 0.043 MHz, vastly less powerful than a modern pocket calculator.

Astronomy Fact — The Great Attractor is a mysterious gravitational anomaly pulling our galaxy and thousands of others toward it at about 600 km per second. It lies hidden behind the plane of the Milky Way.

Biology Fact — A cockroach can survive for about a week without its head, dying mainly of dehydration rather than the decapitation itself. Its decentralized nervous system and open circulatory system let it function briefly.

Physics Fact — The deepest note a pipe organ can produce vibrates around 16 hertz, near the lower limit of human hearing. Below that, you feel the sound as pressure in your body rather than hear it with your ears.

Chemistry Fact — Antifreeze lowers water's freezing point through a phenomenon called freezing point depression. Adding any solute disrupts the formation of the ice crystal lattice, so salty seawater also freezes below zero.

History Fact — The Haitian Revolution, led by formerly enslaved people, culminated in independence in 1804. Haiti became the first nation founded by a successful slave revolt and the second independent republic in the Americas.
Math Fact — Honeybees build hexagonal honeycombs because the hexagon tiles a plane using the least wall material for a given area. This honeycomb conjecture was proven by Thomas Hales in 1999.
Art & Tech Fact — The bust of Nefertiti, sculpted around 1345 BCE by the artist Thutmose, has one finished quartz-inlaid eye and one left blank; it was unearthed in Egypt in 1912.
Astronomy Fact — Mars has the largest dust storms in the Solar System, sometimes engulfing the entire planet for months. A 2018 global storm ended NASA's solar-powered Opportunity rover.

Biology Fact — The bar-headed goose migrates over the Himalayas, flying at altitudes near 7,000 meters where oxygen is scarce. Its hemoglobin binds oxygen far more efficiently than that of related lowland species.

Physics Fact — When you wring a wet towel in space, the water does not fly off but clings to your hands and the fabric in floating blobs. Without gravity, surface tension dominates and holds the water in place.

Chemistry Fact — Fluorine is the most reactive element of all. It reacts with almost everything, even setting steel wool and asbestos on fire, and it forms compounds with noble gases that resist all other reactants.

History Fact — The eruption of Mount Tambora in present-day Indonesia in 1815 was so powerful that 1816 became known as the 'Year Without a Summer.' Crop failures across the Northern Hemisphere caused widespread famine.
Math Fact — The sum 1 + 2 + 3 + ... + 100 equals 5050. Legend says young Gauss found it instantly by pairing numbers from both ends, each pair summing to 101.
Art & Tech Fact — Photoshop was created by brothers Thomas and John Knoll, starting in 1987; Adobe licensed it and shipped version 1.0 in 1990, initially only for the Apple Macintosh.
Astronomy Fact — The Sun is about 109 times wider than Earth, so roughly 1.3 million Earths could fit inside it. Yet it is just an average-sized star.

Biology Fact — Coral reefs are built by tiny animals living in symbiosis with photosynthetic algae called zooxanthellae. When stressed by heat, corals expel these algae and turn white, an event known as coral bleaching.

Physics Fact — The faster you move, the slower your clock ticks relative to a stationary observer. GPS satellites must correct for this effect, or their position calculations would drift by several kilometers per day.

Chemistry Fact — The color of fireworks comes from metal salts: strontium for red, barium for green, copper for blue, and sodium for yellow. Each element emits light at characteristic wavelengths when heated.

History Fact — The first photograph from nature, 'View from the Window at Le Gras,' was made by Nicéphore Niépce around 1826 or 1827. The exposure took several hours, so the sunlight appears to fall on multiple sides of the buildings.

Math Fact — A googol is 10 to the 100th power, larger than the number of atoms in the observable universe. The googolplex, 10 to the googol, cannot be fully written even using every particle in existence.
Art & Tech Fact — The Bayeux Tapestry, depicting the 1066 Norman conquest of England, is actually an embroidery about 70 meters long and includes 626 human figures and over 200 horses.

Astronomy Fact — Some exoplanets orbit so close to their stars that a year lasts only hours. The planet 55 Cancri e completes an orbit in about 18 hours and may have surface temperatures over 2,000 degrees Celsius.
Biology Fact — The human eye can detect a single photon of light under ideal dark-adapted conditions. Specialized rod cells in the retina are among the most sensitive light detectors known in nature.

Physics Fact — A material can be both a good electrical conductor and a poor thermal conductor, but most metals are good at both. The Wiedemann-Franz law links the two because the same free electrons carry charge and heat.
Chemistry Fact — Honey never spoils because its extreme sugar concentration draws water out of any microbes by osmosis. Edible honey has been found in ancient Egyptian tombs thousands of years old.

History Fact — The London-to-Birmingham railway and others fueled 'Railway Mania' in 1840s Britain, a speculative bubble in railway shares. When it burst, many middle-class investors, reportedly including writers and clergy, lost fortunes.
Math Fact — Perfect numbers equal the sum of their proper divisors: 6 is 1+2+3, and 28 is 1+2+4+7+14. Every known perfect number is even, and whether any odd ones exist is unsolved.

Art & Tech Fact — The first text message was sent in December 1992 by engineer Neil Papworth, who typed 'Merry Christmas' on a computer and sent it to a colleague's Orbitel phone over the Vodafone network.

Astronomy Fact — The Moon causes Earth's tides, but it also raises tides in the solid Earth itself, flexing the ground by up to about 30 cm twice a day. You rise and fall slightly without ever noticing.

Biology Fact — Dragonflies are among the most successful predators in nature, catching prey on more than 95 percent of attempts. Their brains predict where prey will be and intercept it mid-air rather than simply chasing it.

Physics Fact — Snowflakes form six-fold symmetric shapes because water molecules bond at angles set by their hydrogen bonding geometry. No two are likely identical because each grows through a unique path of temperature and humidity.

Chemistry Fact — Graphene is a single layer of carbon atoms arranged in a hexagonal grid. It is about 200 times stronger than steel by weight, conducts electricity superbly, and is essentially transparent.

History Fact — The transcontinental telegraph completed in the United States in October 1861 made the Pony Express obsolete almost overnight. The famous mail service had operated for only about 18 months.
Math Fact — The harmonic series, 1 + 1/2 + 1/3 + 1/4 and so on, diverges to infinity despite its terms shrinking to zero. Removing every fraction containing the digit 9, however, makes it converge.

Art & Tech Fact — Georgia O'Keeffe's large-scale flower paintings of the 1920s were partly a response to the new dominance of skyscrapers and photography; she said she enlarged blooms so busy New Yorkers would be forced to notice them.

Astronomy Fact — Quasars are among the brightest objects in the universe, powered by supermassive black holes devouring matter. A single quasar can outshine an entire galaxy of hundreds of billions of stars.

Biology Fact — Lichens are not single organisms but partnerships between fungi and algae or cyanobacteria. This alliance lets them colonize bare rock, survive in deserts and the Arctic, and pioneer soil formation.

Physics Fact — The pressure at the bottom of the Mariana Trench is over 1,000 times atmospheric pressure, around 1,100 bar. That force could crush an unprotected human almost instantly, yet specialized creatures thrive there.
Chemistry Fact — Acids and bases neutralize each other by exchanging protons, producing water and a salt. Mixing baking soda and vinegar fizzes because the reaction releases carbon dioxide gas.

History Fact — Abraham Lincoln's Gettysburg Address in November 1863 lasted only about two minutes and ran roughly 270 words. The featured orator that day, Edward Everett, had spoken for about two hours beforehand.
Math Fact — There are exactly five Platonic solids: the tetrahedron, cube, octahedron, dodecahedron, and icosahedron. Euclid proved this is all there can be, a fact dictated purely by geometry.
Art & Tech Fact — The Commodore 64, released in 1982, is often cited as the best-selling single computer model of all time, with estimates ranging from about 12.5 to 17 million units sold.
Astronomy Fact — The asteroid belt between Mars and Jupiter is mostly empty space. If you combined all its asteroids, their total mass would be less than that of Earth's Moon.

Biology Fact — Whale falls create entire ecosystems on the deep seafloor that can persist for decades. The sinking carcass feeds scavengers, then bacteria, and finally specialized worms that mine the lipid-rich bones.

Physics Fact — Light exerts a tiny but real pressure when it strikes a surface, called radiation pressure. Solar sails use this push from sunlight to propel spacecraft without any fuel, gradually building up significant speed.

Chemistry Fact — Plutonium is warm to the touch because its radioactive decay constantly releases heat. A few kilograms can stay noticeably warm for decades, a property used to power deep-space probes.

History Fact — The Suez Canal opened in 1869, connecting the Mediterranean and Red Seas and dramatically shortening the sea route between Europe and Asia. It was built largely with conscripted Egyptian labor over about a decade.
Math Fact — The Pythagorean theorem was known to Babylonians a thousand years before Pythagoras. A clay tablet from around 1800 BCE lists Pythagorean triples with remarkable sophistication.
Art & Tech Fact — The 'Venus de Milo,' carved around 100 BCE and found on the Greek island of Milos in 1820, was likely depicted holding an apple; her arms were already missing when discovered.

Astronomy Fact — Light does not always travel in a straight line; massive objects bend it. This 'gravitational lensing' lets telescopes use galaxy clusters as natural magnifying lenses to see more distant galaxies.

Biology Fact — Your brain uses about 20 percent of your body's energy despite being only about 2 percent of your weight. Most of that energy maintains the constant electrical signaling between billions of neurons.

Physics Fact — A spinning top stays upright as long as it spins fast enough, but as friction slows it, it begins to wobble in a slow circle called precession. The same precession makes Earth's axis trace a circle every 26,000 years.

Chemistry Fact — The element with the highest known electronegativity is fluorine, meaning it pulls bonding electrons toward itself more strongly than any other atom. This drives much of fluorine's ferocious reactivity.

History Fact — Krakatoa erupted in 1883 with such force that the sound was reportedly heard nearly 5,000 kilometers away. The blast is considered one of the loudest events in recorded history, and barometers worldwide registered the pressure wave.

Math Fact — Negative numbers were long called fictitious or absurd by European mathematicians. They gained full acceptance only in the 1700s, despite Chinese mathematicians using them centuries earlier with red and black rods.
Art & Tech Fact — The compact disc was co-developed by Sony and Philips and launched commercially in 1982; its 74-minute capacity was reportedly chosen so it could hold Beethoven's Ninth Symphony.
Astronomy Fact — A full Moon is about 14 times brighter than a half Moon, not twice as bright. The rough lunar surface scatters far more light back at us when the Sun is directly behind us.

Biology Fact — The horseshoe crab has copper-based blue blood prized by medicine because it clots in the presence of bacterial toxins. This property is used to test vaccines and medical devices for contamination.

Physics Fact — Static electricity can build up to tens of thousands of volts on your body from walking across carpet. The spark you feel is a tiny lightning bolt jumping across the air gap to a grounded object.

Chemistry Fact — Ammonia for fertilizer is made by combining nitrogen from air with hydrogen under enormous pressure, the Haber-Bosch process. This single reaction is estimated to sustain roughly half the world's food supply.
History Fact — The Eiffel Tower was completed in 1889 for the World's Fair and was meant to stand for only 20 years. It was saved because its height made it useful as a radiotelegraph antenna.
Math Fact — The decimal 0.999... repeating exactly equals 1, not just approximately. One proof: one-third equals 0.333..., and multiplying both sides by 3 gives 1 equals 0.999...

Art & Tech Fact — Henri Matisse, confined to a wheelchair late in life, turned to 'painting with scissors,' creating large cut-paper collages; his celebrated work 'The Snail' from 1953 is built entirely from cut colored shapes.

Astronomy Fact — The coldest known place in the universe so far is the Boomerang Nebula at about 1 degree above absolute zero. Rapidly expanding gas chills it below even the cosmic background temperature.

Biology Fact — Salmon navigate back to their birth stream partly by smell, detecting the unique chemical signature of their home waters. Some species die shortly after spawning, their bodies fertilizing the ecosystem.

Physics Fact — The speed of light in a vacuum, about 299,792 kilometers per second, is the universe's ultimate speed limit. As an object approaches it, the energy needed to accelerate further grows toward infinity.

Chemistry Fact — Methane is a far more potent greenhouse gas than carbon dioxide over short timescales, trapping dozens of times more heat per molecule. Fortunately it also breaks down in the atmosphere within about a decade.

History Fact — In 1898 Marie and Pierre Curie discovered polonium and radium. Marie Curie went on to win Nobel Prizes in two different sciences, physics in 1903 and chemistry in 1911, a feat no other woman has matched.

Math Fact — Imaginary numbers, built on the square root of negative one, were once dismissed as nonsense yet are now essential to electrical engineering and quantum mechanics. The name imaginary was meant as an insult.
